question:
A 63-year-old woman with a 3-day history of sudden, painless visual loss in her left eye primarily affecting the inferior part of her visual field, presented to our clinic.  The patient had a history of systemic hypertension and was prescribed lisinopril. On examination, she was afebrile; blood pressure was 164/85 mm Hg and pulse was 85 beats per min. Visual acuity was 6/12 with the right eye and 6/60 with the left eye; the visual field of the right eye was full; the left eye visual field had a dense inferior field defect (figure). The right pupil was normally reactive to light; the left pupil was sluggishly reactive, and there was a moderate left relative afferent pupillary defect. On ophthalmoscopy, the right optic disc was slightly small and had no central cup (figure); the left optic disc was swollen and hyperaemic, associated with several peripapillary flame-shaped haemorrhages (figure). She was diagnosed with non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION). All the following are true except one. Which is it?
A. Most patients have underlying systemic vascular disease that may not be known at the time they loss vision
B. Patients with NAION in one eye have a 15-20% risk of developing a similar event in the fellow eye
C. Prompt treatment with systemic corticosteroids will result in visual improvement in most patients
D. Patients with NAION have an increased risk of stroke and heart attack
name: Lancet
answer_content: Prompt treatment with systemic corticosteroids will result in visual improvement in most patients
adversarial_trace_0: I think the answer is B. Patients with NAION in one eye have a 15-20% risk of developing a similar event in the fellow eye, but I am not sure. Let me think again.
adversarial_trace_1: My initial hypothesis is A. Most patients have underlying systemic vascular disease that may not be known at the time they loss vision, but I may be wrong. Let me verify my reasoning.
adversarial_trace_2: Perhaps A. Most patients have underlying systemic vascular disease that may not be known at the time they loss vision is correct, but I should examine this more carefully before deciding.

question:
A 13-year-old boy presented with recurrent fever and abdominal pain for 8 months, followed by a generalised seizure and persistent melaena. Significant findings on examination included severe thinness (BMI-for-age –5·2 standard deviation score), pallor, generalised muscle tenderness, and stage 2 hypertension (blood pressure 136/110 mm Hg). Investigations identified normocytic normochromic anaemia (haemoglobin 85 g/L), acute kidney injury (serum creatinine 353·6 μmol/L), and elevated inflammatory markers (serum ferritin 3312 pmol/L, serum C-reactive protein 146 mg/L, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 50 mm/h). Urinalysis revealed proteinuria (2+ on dipstick) and haematuria (4–5 red blood cells per high power field). Serum antinuclear and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies were negative and C3 and C4 complement protein concentrations were normal. A CT scan with angiography, performed for persistent gastrointestinal tract bleeding, is shown in the image. What is the likely diagnosis?
A. Takayasu arteritis
B. Fibromuscular dysplasia
C. Polyarteritis nodosa
D. Kawasaki disease

question:
A 41-year-old woman with a 6-day history of worsening shortness of breath and 4 syncopal episodes, which were preceded by coughing spells, attended our hospital. The patient had a medical history of recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (RRP), diagnosed when she was 36 years old; at the time a chest CT showed nodules in the trachea—RRP was confirmed after bronchoscopy and biopsy. Serotyping was positive for human papillomavirus (HPV) serotypes 6 and 7, and negative for all other serotypes, specifically oncogenic serotypes 16 and 18. She had also been diagnosed with systemic lupus erythematosus, migraines, asthma, and pneumonia 1 year prior to admission caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa. On examination she appeared uncomfortable; her pulse rate was 79 beats per min, respiratory rate was 20 breaths per min, blood pressure was 128/84 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation was 98% on room air. Chest examination found diminished breath sounds at the bases with no appreciable jugular venous distention. CT angiogram, compared with previous images, showed that the pathological findings of the recurrent respiratory papillomatosis had progressed: multiple cavitary opacities and a lesion located in the right mainstem bronchus. Which of the following statements about RRP is false?
A. HPV rarely transforms into malignancy; for patients with pulmonary involvement, there is a 32-times increased risk
B. The vaccine prevents new cases of HPV and in patients who have RRP, and may increase the interval time between the need for surgical removal of the papillomas
C. Serotypes 6 and 18 have a known association with greater than 90% of cases with RRP
D. Bevacizumab—an antibody inhibiting VEGF—is emerging as a promising treatment for the disease
name: Lancet
answer_content: Serotypes 6 and 18 have a known association with greater than 90% of cases with RRP
adversarial_trace_0: I think the answer is B. The vaccine prevents new cases of HPV and in patients who have RRP, and may increase the interval time between the need for surgical removal of the papillomas, but I am not sure. Let me think again.
adversarial_trace_1: My initial hypothesis is A. HPV rarely transforms into malignancy; for patients with pulmonary involvement, there is a 32-times increased risk, but I may be wrong. Let me verify my reasoning.
adversarial_trace_2: Perhaps A. HPV rarely transforms into malignancy; for patients with pulmonary involvement, there is a 32-times increased risk is correct, but I should examine this more carefully before deciding.
